stage: Stage 1 trajectory normalization and sampling sensitivity
research_question: Does deterministic arc-length normalization reduce sampling-density dependence in geometry-derived primitives and topology candidates, or introduce new distortion?
input_condition: Two metric sports trajectories; raw descriptors/primitives/topology compared with arc-length resampling steps 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 m; stop candidates kept raw-only.
main_finding: Primitive counts and locations are step-sensitive; resampling exposes sampling dependence and preserves source traceability, but does not establish a production default.
supported_claim: Paper A can say sampling density materially affects descriptor-derived candidate artifacts and that arc-length resampling is useful as an experimental sensitivity probe.
limitation: n=2 samples; arbitrary 50 m / 10 s gap guards; linear chord interpolation; no smoothing, perturbation framework, topology persistence test, or production default step.
safe_manuscript_wording: Arc-length resampling was used as a staged sensitivity analysis; it characterizes sampling-dependent behavior and does not define a canonical preprocessing policy.

research_question: Does segment-first arc-length resampling better respect gap boundaries than global resampling, or introduce segment-boundary distortion?
input_condition: Two metric sports trajectories; gap split at distance >50 m or dt >10 s; per-segment exact-grid resampling and per-segment descriptors/primitives/topology.
main_finding: Segment-first processing reduced running topology event counts at 1 m from 3972 to 1574 and preserved no-cross-gap computation, but this may remove real cross-segment structure as well as reduce artifacts.
supported_claim: Paper A can say segment boundaries strongly affect candidate counts and that gap-aware resampling is an experimental design axis.
limitation: Gap thresholds uncalibrated; per-segment topology can lose cross-segment relations; n=2; 2 m is not a default; no ground truth decides whether reduced counts are better.
safe_manuscript_wording: Segment-first resampling demonstrates a stability boundary around gap handling; lower candidate counts are not evidence of more correct detection.

research_question: Does the experimental segment_resample API produce internally consistent metadata on real samples and step sweeps?
input_condition: The segment_resample src API was run on skiing_run_01 and running_2026-04-09 at steps 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 m.
main_finding: Validation status passed; skiing produced 2 segments and 1 gap, running produced 21 segments and 20 gaps; metadata was internally consistent across tested steps.
supported_claim: Paper A can cite the resampling API as an implemented experimental tool with validated metadata consistency.
limitation: This is internal smoke validation only; no geometric truth claim, no sampling policy, and no external validation.
safe_manuscript_wording: The segment-resample API passed internal metadata consistency checks on two samples, supporting its use as an experimental sensitivity tool only.

research_question: Can segment_resample produce consistent outputs for sparse and dense observations of the same synthetic geometry?
input_condition: Synthetic straight line, L-shape, arc, and gap cases; sparse vs dense inputs resampled at 2 m and compared on geometry, descriptors, and turn-boundary proxies.
main_finding: Straight line, L-shape, and gap cases matched after resampling; the arc case exposed chord-approximation limits with 0.5742 m RMS position difference and 34.0841 degrees cumulative-turn delta.
supported_claim: Paper A can say resampling reduces sampling-density dependence for simple piecewise-linear/gap cases but exposes limits on curved sparse observations.
limitation: Synthetic only; turn-boundary proxies are not primitive extractor outputs; no real-sample invariance claim; arc failure shows observed chords limit recoverable geometry.
safe_manuscript_wording: Sampling-density normalization behaved consistently for simple synthetic structures, while curved sparse observations retained approximation error.

research_question: Do deterministic smoothers reduce coordinate jitter artifacts without masking real geometry?
input_condition: Synthetic straight line, L-shape, and arc with sigma=1.0 m jitter; moving average, Gaussian, and Savitzky-Golay smoothing compared against clean geometry.
main_finding: All smoothers improved position RMS, but structural metrics remained biased; Gaussian still left cumulative-turn deltas of 286 degrees on straight, 47 degrees on L-shape, and 183 degrees on arc.
supported_claim: Paper A can say smoothing is not transparent to geometry and that unsmoothed/no-silent-smoothing limits must be explicit.
limitation: Synthetic sigma=1.0 m trial only; no production smoothing policy from this table alone; smoothing changes structure and does not validate candidate correctness.
safe_manuscript_wording: Smoothing reduced positional noise in the trial but did not restore clean structural descriptors, so Paper A treats smoothing as a sensitivity issue rather than a solved preprocessing step.

research_question: How much coordinate noise and nonuniform sampling affect the frozen curvature estimator?
input_condition: Synthetic circle, straight line, and sine wave cases; noise sweep sigma=0.1 to 2.0 m; nonuniform 30 percent decimation comparison.
main_finding: Noise strongly inflated apparent curvature; at sigma=1.0 m a straight line had RMSE 1.7191 rad/m and bias +1.3559 rad/m; nonuniform decimation increased sine RMSE from 0.0722 to 0.2199 rad/m, far below the sigma=0.5 jitter RMSE 2.7760 rad/m.
supported_claim: Paper A can say the curvature estimator is deterministic and its sensitivity limits are measured on synthetic cases.
limitation: Fixed-seed synthetic probe; not a calibrated noise-response curve; estimator sensitivity does not validate true movement curvature.
safe_manuscript_wording: The curvature estimator is explicit and deterministic, but curvature-derived candidates inherit a measured noise floor under coordinate jitter.

research_question: How stable are primitive candidate counts and boundaries under coordinate jitter?
input_condition: Synthetic L-shape, straight, stop, out-and-back, and arc tracks; sigma=0, 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 m jitter; existing deterministic extractors only.
main_finding: Candidate counts were sensitive to noise: straight turn candidates appeared at sigma=0.1 m, stop was missed at sigma>=0.5 m, and out-and-back reversal counts rose from 1 clean to 10 at sigma=1.0 m.
supported_claim: Paper A can say primitive outputs are threshold-conditioned candidates and confidence is not perturbation-based.
limitation: Synthetic cases only; order-matched boundary shifts are weak when counts differ; thresholds are not calibrated to real GNSS noise.
safe_manuscript_wording: Primitive candidates persist or fail depending on noise and thresholds; confidence scores remain heuristic and should not be read as survival probabilities.

research_question: How do topology candidate counts, intersection locations, and false positives change under tolerance and jitter sweeps?
input_condition: Synthetic near-miss, figure-8, closed-loop, overlap, and straight-line cases; tolerance sweep and sigma=0.1, 0.5, 1.0 m jitter.
main_finding: Near-miss detection occurred when tolerance reached the 0.05 m gap; figure-8 proper crossing persisted to sigma=1.0 m with 0.8708 m point shift; endpoint touches and overlaps were fragile; straight jitter produced 2 false positives at sigma=1.0 m.
supported_claim: Paper A can say topology extraction is deterministic but tolerance-conditioned and sensitive to coordinate perturbation.
limitation: Synthetic cases only; event counts include paired self_intersection_candidate and loop_candidate representations, not unique crossings; no graph persistence metric.
safe_manuscript_wording: Topology outputs are tolerance-conditioned candidates; tolerance is an observation parameter, not a ground-truth boundary.
